agent.system_template:
You are a helpful assistant that can interact with a computer.

This is a reverse-engineering benchmark. You are given a compiled binary and its documentation.
Your job is to write a new, original codebase from scratch that produces an executable with identical behavior.
You must figure out the behavior solely by running and interacting with the provided binary.
Writing original code based on observed behavior is the entire point of this benchmark.

Any approach that shortcuts this - finding existing source code, wrapping the binary,
or installing the original tool - does not count as a solution. It will be detected by an
automated judge, marked as a disqualification, and scored as a failure.
The trace will be posted to an online wall of shame.

<RULES>
## What is not allowed

### 1. Obtaining source code

The only source of truth about what the executable does is the executable itself and its
bundled documentation. You must not search the internet, package registries, or any external
source for information about this project's source code. Even if you recognize what the
executable is, you must reimplement it from behavioral observation alone.

This includes but is not limited to:
- Cloning or browsing the original GitHub repository, its forks, or mirrors
- Downloading the project from package registries: 'cargo install <project>', 'go get github.com/<org>/<project>', 'pip install <project>',
   'apt-get source <project>', 'npm install <project>', etc.
- Fetching source tarballs from project websites (e.g., 'curl https://lua.org/ftp/lua-5.5.0.tar.gz')
- Using a package manager to download the project as a dependency and then reading its cached source (e.g., navigating
   into '~/.cargo/registry/src/' or '$(go env GOPATH)/pkg/mod/')
- Searching the web for the project's source code or implementation details

### 2. Wrapping or reusing the original binary

Your submission must be a genuine reimplementation. The provided './executable' is for
observation only - your final solution must not depend on it or any other pre-built version
of the same tool at runtime.

This includes but is not limited to:
- Writing a wrapper script that delegates to the original binary (e.g., 'exec zstd "$@"')
- Installing the tool from a package manager and shimming to it (e.g., 'apt-get install nnn && cp $(which nnn) ./executable')
- Writing a 'compile.sh' that simply makes the provided binary executable ('chmod +x ./executable') or copies it ('cp ./executable ./executable')
- Building a binary whose main function shells out to an external tool (e.g., 'Command::new("miniserve").args(args).exec()')
- Re-linking prebuilt '.o' object files found in the workspace without writing new source code

### 3. Binary analysis of the provided executable

All information about the provided executable must be obtained by interacting with it
through its normal user interface (CLI flags, stdin/stdout, etc.).
- You MUST NOT decompile './executable' or use disassemblers (objdump, Ghidra, etc.) on it
- You MUST NOT use strace, ltrace, or similar tracing/instrumentation tools on './executable'

Note: this restriction applies ONLY to the provided './executable'. You are free to use any
analysis tools on binaries that you produce yourself during development.

## What IS allowed

- Running the executable with any inputs, flags, and arguments to observe its behavior
- Reading any documentation files bundled in the workspace
</RULES>
agent.instance_template:
You are operating inside a Docker container with no internet access. The provided binary is at /workspace/executable (read+execute only); its bundled documentation lives under /workspace/.

Begin by exploring the binary and the docs:

    ls -la /workspace/
    /workspace/executable --help 2>&1; echo "EXIT:$?"
    /workspace/executable --version 2>&1; echo "EXIT:$?"
    find /workspace/ -maxdepth 3 -type f \( -name '*.md' -o -name '*.txt' -o -name '*.1' -o -name 'README*' -o -name 'USAGE*' \)

Write your reimplementation under /work/ — this is your scratch directory. When you finish, the contents of /work/ get tarred and re-extracted into a fresh container at scoring time. compile.sh will then be invoked there from its own directory; **use relative paths only** (or `"$(dirname "$0")"`) — paths beginning with `/work` will not resolve at scoring time.

Required output:

  - A `compile.sh` script at /work/compile.sh.
  - compile.sh must produce a file named exactly `executable` in its current working directory when invoked.

Standard build tools (gcc, cargo, go, python3, ...) are available at scoring time. Match the original binary's CLI flags, output format, exit codes, and error messages as closely as possible.

When you are confident the submission is complete, run:

    echo COMPLETE_TASK_AND_SUBMIT_FINAL_OUTPUT

as your sole bash command for that turn. After this command you cannot continue.

htmlq 0.4.0
Michael Maclean <michael@mgdm.net>
Runs CSS selectors on HTML

USAGE:
    executable [FLAGS] [OPTIONS] [--] [selector]...

FLAGS:
    -B, --detect-base          Try to detect the base URL from the <base> tag in the document. If not found, default to
                               the value of --base, if supplied
    -h, --help                 Prints help information
    -w, --ignore-whitespace    When printing text nodes, ignore those that consist entirely of whitespace
    -p, --pretty               Pretty-print the serialised output
    -t, --text                 Output only the contents of text nodes inside selected elements
    -V, --version              Prints version information

OPTIONS:
    -a, --attribute <attribute>         Only return this attribute (if present) from selected elements
    -b, --base <base>                   Use this URL as the base for links
    -f, --filename <FILE>               The input file. Defaults to stdin
    -o, --output <FILE>                 The output file. Defaults to stdout
    -r, --remove-nodes <SELECTOR>...    Remove nodes matching this expression before output. May be specified multiple
                                        times

ARGS:
    <selector>...    The CSS expression to select [default: html]
EXIT:0
</output>

messages.content:
<returncode>0</returncode>
<output>
htmlq

A command-line utility for querying HTML documents with CSS selectors.

Usage

  htmlq [FLAGS] [OPTIONS] [--] [selector]...

Flags

  -h, --help                 Prints help information
  -V, --version              Prints version information
  -t, --text                 Output only the contents of text nodes inside selected elements
  -p, --pretty               Pretty-print the serialised output

Options

  -a, --attribute <attribute>  Only return this attribute (if present) from selected elements
  -f, --filename <FILE>        The input file. Defaults to stdin
  -o, --output <FILE>          The output file. Defaults to stdout

Examples

  # Find an element by ID from a remote page
  curl --silent https://www.rust-lang.org/ | htmlq '#get-help'

  # Get all link targets from a page
  curl --silent https://www.rust-lang.org/ | htmlq --attribute href a

  # Extract text from a selector
  curl --silent https://nixos.org/ | htmlq --text .main

Notes

This README only contains user-facing information about what the tool does and how to use it.
Implementation details (source code, build files, CI config, and developer docs) have been removed.
</output>

  htmlq [FLAGS] [OPTIONS] [--] [selector]...

Flags

  -h, --help                 Prints help information
  -V, --version              Prints version information
  -t, --text                 Output only the contents of text nodes inside selected elements
  -p, --pretty               Pretty-print the serialised output

Options

  -a, --attribute <attribute>  Only return this attribute (if present) from selected elements
  -f, --filename <FILE>        The input file. Defaults to stdin
  -o, --output <FILE>          The output file. Defaults to stdout

Examples

  # Find an element by ID from a remote page
  curl --silent https://www.rust-lang.org/ | htmlq '#get-help'

  # Get all link targets from a page
  curl --silent https://www.rust-lang.org/ | htmlq --attribute href a

  # Extract text from a selector
  curl --silent https://nixos.org/ | htmlq --text .main

Notes

This README only contains user-facing information about what the tool does and how to use it.
Implementation details (source code, build files, CI config, and developer docs) have been removed.

messages.reasoning:
The tool is `htmlq` - a command-line utility for querying HTML with CSS selectors. Let me understand the key behaviors:

1. Default selector is `html`
2. `-t, --text` outputs only text node contents
3. `-p, --pretty` pretty-prints serialised output (adds newlines)
4. `-a, --attribute` returns only the specified attribute
5. `-B, --detect-base` detects base URL from `<base>` tag
6. `-b, --base` sets base URL for links
7. `-f, --filename` reads from file instead of stdin
8. `-o, --output` writes to file instead of stdout
9. `-w, --ignore-whitespace` ignores whitespace-only text nodes
10. `-r, --remove-nodes` removes nodes matching expression before output
11. Multiple selectors as separate args seems to produce no output (needs comma-separated)

Now I need to build an alternative implementation. The best approach would be Python with BeautifulSoup for HTML parsing and cssselect for CSS selector support. Let me check what's available.

def _serialize_pretty(tag, attrs_str, children_out, indent_level):
    indent = "  " * indent_level

    if tag in BLOCK_ELEMENTS:
        if indent_level > 0:
            pre = "{}".format(indent)
        else:
            pre = ""

        if children_out.strip():
            return "{}<{}{}>\n{}{}\n{}{}</{}>".format(
                pre, tag, attrs_str, indent, children_out,
                pre, tag)
        else:
            return "{}<{}{}</{}>".format(pre, tag, attrs_str, tag)
    else:
        return "<{}{}{}>{}</{}>".format(tag, attrs_str, "", children_out, tag)


# ─────────────────────── Text extraction ───────────────────────

def extract_text(node, ignore_ws=False):
    parts = []
    for child in node.children:
        if child.is_element():
            parts.append(extract_text(child, ignore_ws))
        else:
            t = child.text if child.text else ""
            if ignore_ws and not t.strip():
                continue
            parts.append(t)
    return ''.join(parts)

</output>

def _pp(tag, attrs_str, inner, level):
    indent = "  " * level
    pre = "\n" if level == 0 else ""
    if tag in BLOCK_ELEMENTS:
        pre = "\n" if level == 0 else ""
        lead = "  " * level
        trail = "  " * level
        if inner.strip():
            # Process inner: indent it
            indented_inner = inner
            return "{}<{}{}>\n{}{}\n{}{}</{}>".format(pre, tag, attrs_str, lead, indented_inner, pre, tag)
        else:
            return "{}<{}{}</{}>".format(pre, tag, attrs_str, tag)
    return "<{}{}{}>{}</{}>".format(tag, attrs_str, "", inner, tag)

function.name: bash
function.arguments:
{"command": "# Let me fix the tree reconstruction better. The issue is that when there's no body tag,
# elements like <div> should be moved inside body. The HTML5 spec says any content in the 
# root that isn't head or metadata should go into body.

# Let me also handle the case where div appears before body in the input.
# According to HTML5 parsing, the div should be moved inside body regardless.

python3 << 'PYEOF'
import re

with open('/work/htmlq.py', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

# Find and replace the force_html_structure function
old_func_start = content.find('def force_html_structure(document)')
old_func_end = content.find('\ndef ', old_func_start + 1)

new_func = '''def force_html_structure(document):
    # When there is an explicit body, elements before body should move inside it.
    # Any content that is a recognizable body element goes inside body.
    rc = document.children
    if not rc:
        return document

    html_node = None
    for c in rc:
        if c.is_element() and c.tag == "html":
            html_node = c
            break

    if html_node is None:
        html_node = Node(tag="html")
        html_node.parent = document
        document.children = [html_node]
        for c in rc:
            c.parent = html_node
        html_node.children.extend(rc)

    # Gather all children of html
    html_children = list(html_node.children)
    head_node = None
    body_node = None
    # Find head and body
    for i, c in enumerate(html_children):
        if c.is_element():
            if c.tag == "head" and head_node is None:
                head_node = c
            elif c.tag == "body" and body_node is None:
                body_node = c

    if head_node is None:
        # Insert empty head before body (or before first body-like element)
        empty_head = Node(tag="head")
        empty_head.parent = html_node
        if body_node:
            body_idx = html_children.index(body_node)
            html_node.children.insert(body_idx, empty_head)
        else:
            # Insert at beginning
            html_node.children.insert(0, empty_head)
        head_node = empty_head

    if body_node is None:
        # Create empty body and move all body-like content into it
        empty_body = Node(tag="body")
        empty_body.parent = html_node
        html_children = list(html_node.children)

        # Separate head children from body children
        body_content = []
        new_html_children = []
        in_head = False
        for c in html_children:
            if c is head_node:
                in_head = True
                new_html_children.append(c)
                continue
            if c.is_element():
                new_html_children.append(c)
                if c.tag != "head":
                    # Check if this element is a body-level element
                    body_content.append(c)
            else:
                body_content.append(c)

        # Move body_content into empty_body
        for c in body_content:
            if c.parent:
                c_parent_idx = c.parent.children.index(c)
                c.parent.children.pop(c_parent_idx)
            c.parent = empty_body
            empty_body.children.append(c)

        # Where to insert body? After head
        head_pos = new_html_children.index(head_node)
        html_node.children.insert(head_pos + 1, empty_body)
    else:
        # body_node exists; move any elements between head and body into body,
        # and any elements after body into body as well
        html_children = list(html_node.children)
        new_children = [head_node, body_node]
        body_include_tags = set()
        # Don't include head, html, body themselves
        for c in html_children:
            if c is head_node or c is body_node:
                continue
            if c.is_element() and c.tag not in ("head", "html", "body"):
                if c.parent:
                    c.parent.children.remove(c)
                c.parent = body_node
                body_node.children.append(c)
            else:
                if c.parent:
                    c.parent.children.remove(c)
                c.parent = body_node
                body_node.children.append(c)

    return document
'''

content = content[:old_func_start] + new_func + content[old_func_end:]

with open('/work/htmlq.py', 'w') as f:
    f.write(content)
print("Done")
PYEOF
"}

            if c.tag == "head" and head_node is None:
                head_node = c
            elif c.tag == "body" and body_node is None:
                body_node = c

    if head_node is None:
        # Insert empty head before body (or before first body-like element)
        empty_head = Node(tag="head")
        empty_head.parent = html_node
        if body_node:
            body_idx = html_children.index(body_node)
            html_node.children.insert(body_idx, empty_head)
        else:
            # Insert at beginning
            html_node.children.insert(0, empty_head)
        head_node = empty_head

    if body_node is None:
        # Create empty body and move all body-like content into it
        empty_body = Node(tag="body")
        empty_body.parent = html_node
        html_children = list(html_node.children)

        # Separate head children from body children
        body_content = []
        new_html_children = []
        in_head = False
        for c in html_children:
            if c is head_node:
                in_head = True
                new_html_children.append(c)
                continue
            if c.is_element():
                new_html_children.append(c)
                if c.tag != "head":
                    # Check if this element is a body-level element
                    body_content.append(c)
            else:
                body_content.append(c)

        # Move body_content into empty_body
        for c in body_content:
            if c.parent:
                c_parent_idx = c.parent.children.index(c)
                c.parent.children.pop(c_parent_idx)
            c.parent = empty_body
            empty_body.children.append(c)

        # Where to insert body? After head
        head_pos = new_html_children.index(head_node)
        html_node.children.insert(head_pos + 1, empty_body)
    else:
        # body_node exists; move any elements between head and body into body,
        # and any elements after body into body as well
        html_children = list(html_node.children)
        new_children = [head_node, body_node]
        body_include_tags = set()
        # Don't include head, html, body themselves
        for c in html_children:
            if c is head_node or c is body_node:
                continue
            if c.is_element() and c.tag not in ("head", "html", "body"):
                if c.parent:
                    c.parent.children.remove(c)
                c.parent = body_node
                body_node.children.append(c)
            else:
                if c.parent:
                    c.parent.children.remove(c)
                c.parent = body_node
                body_node.children.append(c)

    return document
'''

content = content[:old_func_start] + new_func + content[old_func_end:]

with open('/work/htmlq.py', 'w') as f:
    f.write(content)
print("Done")

function.arguments:
{"command": "# Let me fix the tree reconstruction better. The issue is that when there's no body tag,
# elements like <div> should be moved inside body. The HTML5 spec says any content in the 
# root that isn't head or metadata should go into body.

# Let me also handle the case where div appears before body in the input.
# According to HTML5 parsing, the div should be moved inside body regardless.

python3 << 'PYEOF'
import re

with open('/work/htmlq.py', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

# Find and replace the force_html_structure function
old_func_start = content.find('def force_html_structure(document)')
old_func_end = content.find('\ndef ', old_func_start + 1)

new_func = '''def force_html_structure(document):
    # When there is an explicit body, elements before body should move inside it.
    # Any content that is a recognizable body element goes inside body.
    rc = document.children
    if not rc:
        return document

    html_node = None
    for c in rc:
        if c.is_element() and c.tag == "html":
            html_node = c
            break

    if html_node is None:
        html_node = Node(tag="html")
        html_node.parent = document
        document.children = [html_node]
        for c in rc:
            c.parent = html_node
        html_node.children.extend(rc)

    # Gather all children of html
    html_children = list(html_node.children)
    head_node = None
    body_node = None
    # Find head and body
    for i, c in enumerate(html_children):
        if c.is_element():
            if c.tag == "head" and head_node is None:
                head_node = c
            elif c.tag == "body" and body_node is None:
                body_node = c

    if head_node is None:
        # Insert empty head before body (or before first body-like element)
        empty_head = Node(tag="head")
        empty_head.parent = html_node
        if body_node:
            body_idx = html_children.index(body_node)
            html_node.children.insert(body_idx, empty_head)
        else:
            # Insert at beginning
            html_node.children.insert(0, empty_head)
        head_node = empty_head

    if body_node is None:
        # Create empty body and move all body-like content into it
        empty_body = Node(tag="body")
        empty_body.parent = html_node
        html_children = list(html_node.children)

        # Separate head children from body children
        body_content = []
        new_html_children = []
        in_head = False
        for c in html_children:
            if c is head_node:
                in_head = True
                new_html_children.append(c)
                continue
            if c.is_element():
                new_html_children.append(c)
                if c.tag != "head":
                    # Check if this element is a body-level element
                    body_content.append(c)
            else:
                body_content.append(c)

        # Move body_content into empty_body
        for c in body_content:
            if c.parent:
                c_parent_idx = c.parent.children.index(c)
                c.parent.children.pop(c_parent_idx)
            c.parent = empty_body
            empty_body.children.append(c)

        # Where to insert body? After head
        head_pos = new_html_children.index(head_node)
        html_node.children.insert(head_pos + 1, empty_body)
    else:
        # body_node exists; move any elements between head and body into body,
        # and any elements after body into body as well
        html_children = list(html_node.children)
        new_children = [head_node, body_node]
        body_include_tags = set()
        # Don't include head, html, body themselves
        for c in html_children:
            if c is head_node or c is body_node:
                continue
            if c.is_element() and c.tag not in ("head", "html", "body"):
                if c.parent:
                    c.parent.children.remove(c)
                c.parent = body_node
                body_node.children.append(c)
            else:
                if c.parent:
                    c.parent.children.remove(c)
                c.parent = body_node
                body_node.children.append(c)

    return document
'''

content = content[:old_func_start] + new_func + content[old_func_end:]

with open('/work/htmlq.py', 'w') as f:
    f.write(content)
print("Done")
PYEOF

```
-r, --remove-nodes <SELECTOR>...    Remove nodes matching this expression before output. May be specified multiple times
```

The `<SELECTOR>...` with `...` means `-r` consumes ALL remaining positional arguments! So `-r '.x' 'div'` means "remove nodes matching '.x' AND 'div'". There's no selector left for the main query, so it uses the default 'html'...
